en.extract:
The black-capped swallow (Atticora pileata) is a species of bird in the family Hirundinidae, the swallows and martins. It is found in Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, and Mexico.


Taxonomy and systematics
The black-capped swallow was described in 1858 as Atticora pileata. For much of the twentieth century and into the twenty-first it was reclassified in genus Notiochelidon. Based in part on a study published in 2005, beginning in 2016 taxonomic systems returned it to Atticora.
The black-capped swallow is monotypic.


Description
The black-capped swallow is about 11.5 to 13.5 cm (4.5 to 5.3 in) long and weighs about 12 g (0.42 oz). The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a glossy black head with brown-speckled white cheeks and throat. Their back is sepia brown, their rump a darker brown, and their uppertail coverts dark sooty brown. Their tail longish, forked, and dull sooty black. Their wing's lesser coverts are black to brownish black, their secondary and inner greater coverts are dark grayish brown, and their flight feathers brownish black. The center of their breast and their belly are white, their flanks grayish brown, and their undertail coverts sooty black or very dark sooty brown. They have a brown iris, a black bill, and brown legs and feet. Juveniles are browner and less glossy than adults with a buffy chin and throat and pale cinnamon edges on their upperparts' feathers.


Distribution and habitat
The black-capped swallow has a disjunct distribution. It is found in the central part of the southern Mexican state of Chiapas, from southwestern Chiapas into most of Guatemala, and from extreme northern El Salvador into western Honduras. It inhabits the interior, edges, and clearings of pine-oak forest, pine forest, and cloudforest in the subtropical and temperate zones. Sources differ on its elevational range. One states it is 1,600 to 3,100 m (5,200 to 10,200 ft) and another says 1,000 to 3,000 m (3,300 to 9,800 ft). A third gives its elevational range outside Mexico as 750 to 2,650 m (2,500 to 8,700 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The black-capped swallow is generally considered a year-round resident. It is known to wander in Chiapas. It apparently has bred in Honduras only since the late 1900s.


Feeding
The black-capped swallow feeds only on insects that it captures in flight, though specific details of its diet are lacking. It sometimes forages in small flocks.


Breeding
The black-capped swallow's breeding season has not been fully defined but appears to span February to April in Mexico and include May and June in El Salvador. It sometimes nests in small colonies. It nests in burrows in earthen banks and in rock crevices. It is not clear whether it can excavate its own burrow or is dependent on other species's abandoned burrows. In the burrow or crevice it makes a cup nest of pine needles, leaves, twigs, and mud that is sometimes lined with feathers. The usual clutch is four eggs. Both sexes build the nest and provision nestlings. The incubation period and time to fledging are not known.


Vocalization
The black-capped swallow's calls "include a buzzy electric pzzzzzeeet! and a more liquid pweeet-pweeet".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the black-capped swallow as being of Least Concern. Its population of at least 50,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. It is under "special protection" in Mexico and considered "uncommon to fairly common" outside that country. The species is "often seen near roads and farm clearings, suggesting that they may benefit from human activities that increase edge habitat".


References


Further reading

The '''black-capped swallow''' ('''''Atticora pileata''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Hirundinidae]], the swallows and martins. It is found in [[Guatemala]], [[El Salvador]], [[Honduras]], and [[Mexico]].<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/swallows/ |title=Swallows |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=17 December 2025 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The black-capped swallow was [[Species description|described]] in 1858 as ''Atticora pileata''.<ref>{{cite journal | last=Gould |first=John | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | title=Descriptions of Two New Species of the Family Hirundinidae |volume=XXVI | pages=355 | date= 1858 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/32271889 |access-date=December 25, 2025 }}</ref> For much of the twentieth century and into the twenty-first it was reclassified in genus ''Notiochelidon''. Based in part on a study published in 2005, beginning in 2016 taxonomic systems returned it to ''[[Atticora]]''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Sheldon | first1=F.H. | last2=Whittingham | first2=L.A. | last3=Moyle | first3=R.G. | last4=Slikas | first4=B. | last5=Winkler | first5=D.W. | year=2005 | title=Phylogeny of swallows (Aves: Hirundinidae) estimated from nuclear and mitochondrial DNA sequencing | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=35 | issue=1 | pages=254–270 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2004.11.008 | pmid=15737595 | ref=none}}</ref><ref name=BCSW-BOW>Rodríguez-Flores, C. I., C. A. Soberanes-González, M. d. C. Arizmendi, and T. S. Schulenberg (2020). Black-capped Swallow (''Atticora pileata''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (T. S. Schulenberg, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.blcswa1.01 retrieved December 25, 2025</ref><ref name=57Supp>R. Terry Chesser, Kevin J. Burns, Carla Cicero, Jon L. Dunn, Andrew W. Kratter, Irby J. Lovette, Pamela C. Rasmussen, J. V. Remsen, Jr., James D. Rising, Douglas F. Stotz, and Kevin Winker. "Fifty-seventh supplement to the American Ornithologists' Union ''Check-list of North American Birds''". ''The Auk'' 2016, vol. 133:544-560 retrieved February 12, 2023</ref><ref name=HBW2016>BirdLife International (2016) Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International digital checklist of the birds of the world. [Old] Version 9. Available at: http://datazone.birdlife.org/userfiles/file/Species/Taxonomy/BirdLife_Checklist_Version_90.zip.</ref><ref name=Clements2018>Clements, J. F., et al. 2018. The eBird/Clements checklist of birds of the world: v2018. </ref><ref name=IOC11.2>Gill, F, D Donsker, and P Rasmussen (Eds). 2021. IOC World Bird List (v 11.2). Doi 10.14344/IOC.ML.11.2.  http://www.worldbirdnames.org/</ref>

The black-capped swallow is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC15.1/>

==Description==

The black-capped swallow is about {{convert|11.5|to|13.5|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and weighs about {{convert|12|g|oz|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a glossy black head with brown-speckled white cheeks and throat. Their back is sepia brown, their rump a darker brown, and their uppertail [[coverts]] dark sooty brown. Their tail longish, forked, and dull sooty black. Their wing's lesser coverts are black to brownish black, their secondary and inner greater coverts are dark grayish brown, and their flight feathers brownish black. The center of their breast and their belly are white, their flanks grayish brown, and their undertail coverts sooty black or very dark sooty brown. They have a brown iris, a black bill, and brown legs and feet. Juveniles are browner and less glossy than adults with a buffy chin and throat and pale cinnamon edges on their upperparts' feathers.<ref name=BCSW-BOW/><ref name=Fagan>{{cite book | last1 =Fagan | first1 =Jesse | last2=Komar |first2=Oliver | title =Field Guide to Birds of Northern Central America | publisher =Houghton Mifflin Harcourt | series =Peterson Field Guides | date =2016 | location =Boston | pages =300–301 | isbn =978-0-544-37326-6 }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The black-capped swallow has a [[disjunct distribution]]. It is found in the central part of the southern Mexican state of [[Chiapas]], from southwestern Chiapas into most of Guatemala, and from extreme northern El Salvador into western Honduras. It inhabits the interior, edges, and clearings of [[pine-oak forest]], pine forest, and [[cloudforest]] in the subtropical and temperate zones.<ref name=BCSW-BOW/><ref name=Fagan/><ref name=AOU1998>{{cite book | last =<!--Not stated--> | first =<!--Not stated--> |  title =Check-list of North American Birds | publisher =American Ornithologists' Union |edition =7th | date =1998 | location =Washington, D.C. | pages =459 }}</ref> Sources differ on its elevational range. One states it is {{convert|1600|to|3100|m|ft|abbr=on}}<ref name=AOU1998/> and another says {{convert|1000|to|3000|m|ft|abbr=on}}<ref>Howell, S. N. G., and S. Webb (1995). ''A Guide to the Birds of Mexico and Northern Central America''. Oxford University Press, New York, NY, USA.</ref>. A third gives its elevational range outside Mexico as {{convert|750|to|2650|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}}.<ref name=Fagan/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The black-capped swallow is generally considered a year-round resident.<ref name=AOU1998/> It is known to wander in Chiapas.<ref name=BCSW-BOW/> It apparently has bred in Honduras only since the late 1900s.<ref name=BCSW-BOW/><ref name=Fagan/>

===Feeding===

The black-capped swallow feeds only on insects that it captures in flight, though specific details of its diet are lacking.<ref name=BCSW-BOW/> It sometimes forages in small flocks.<ref name=Fagan/>

===Breeding===

The black-capped swallow's breeding season has not been fully defined but appears to span February to April in Mexico and include May and June in El Salvador. It sometimes nests in small colonies. It nests in burrows in earthen banks and in rock crevices. It is not clear whether it can excavate its own burrow or is dependent on other species's abandoned burrows. In the burrow or crevice it makes a cup nest of pine needles, leaves, twigs, and mud that is sometimes lined with feathers. The usual clutch is four eggs. Both sexes build the nest and provision nestlings. The incubation period and time to fledging are not known.<ref name=BCSW-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Tachycineta-pileata |species=the black-capped swallow}}
===Vocalization===

The black-capped swallow's calls "include a buzzy electric ''pzzzzzeeet!'' and a more liquid ''pweeet-pweeet''".<ref name=Fagan/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the black-capped swallow as being of Least Concern. Its population of at least 50,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is under "special protection" in Mexico<ref name=BCSW-BOW/> and considered "uncommon to fairly common" outside that country<ref name=Fagan/>. The species is "often seen near roads and farm clearings, suggesting that they may benefit from human activities that increase edge habitat".<ref name=BCSW-BOW/>
